Name: quantization-aware_compression_bounds
Title: Quantization-Aware Compression: Establishing Operational Bounds on Bits-Per-Weight in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: This research aims to establish an information-theoretic framework for quantization-aware model compression that identifies the minimum bits-per-weight needed to maintain predictive sufficiency, thereby providing operational bounds that are currently not well-defined in the literature.
Related Work: While existing works such as 'Successive Pruning for Model Compression via Rate Distortion Theory' and 'Lossy Compression with Gaussian Diffusion' explore lossy compression and some aspects of model compression, they do not explicitly derive quantization strategies aligned with operational bounds on bits-per-weight. This proposal aims to fill this gap by integrating quantization strategies directly into the information-theoretic framework, focusing on task-relevant distortion and predictive accuracy.
Abstract: Model compression techniques are essential for deploying deep learning models in resource-constrained environments. This proposal introduces a novel approach to model compression based on quantization-aware strategies framed within an information-theoretic context. We will establish achievable rates and converse bounds on the bits-per-weight required to ensure predictive sufficiency based on various quantization schemes. By treating quantization as a lossy source coding problem under a distortion constraint, we will derive operational bounds that quantify the trade-offs between compression efficiency and predictive performance. Our approach will leverage mutual information and description length principles to allocate bits effectively where they reduce task-relevant distortion. The results will be validated through a series of compression experiments on standard neural network architectures, comparing the performance to existing benchmarks. This research will contribute to a clearer understanding of the fundamental limits of model compression and guide future efforts in efficient neural network design.
Experiments: Conduct experiments on various neural networks (e.g., CNNs, transformers) with different quantization levels.
Evaluate the predictive performance (accuracy) and compression rates (bits-per-weight) to establish a relationship between quantization levels and operational bounds.
Use mutual information as a metric to evaluate task-relevant distortion and derive achievable rate-distortion curves.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds may not fully translate into practice, leading to challenges in empirical validation.
Quantization strategies may introduce biases that could affect the results, requiring careful selection and analysis.
The complexity of neural networks may limit the generalizability of the derived bounds across different architectures.
